titleInfo.title: Multiple Traditions in One Ritual: A Reading of the Lantern-Lighting Prayers in Dunhuang Manuscripts

mods.abstract:
randeng wen 燃燈文 lantern-lighting prayers in Dunhuang manuscripts; zhaiwen 齋文 texts for feasts
In "redefining the lantern-lighting prayers", the author attempts to define this genre without sucess: In sum, short prescriptive (he does not know this word) texts on lighting lanterns are found in many rituals; here, he only considers rituals focusing on lighting lamps alone, and these are in Dunhuang always connected to New Year celebrations. The author translates P.2058 (6), bad translation, and gives an "analysis". He translates (part of?) P.2588. He says that S.2850 and P.3269, which are titled "lantern-lighting prayers" are no such prayers at all. "Three traditions in the Latern-lighting prayers": Lamp as a symbol of wisdom; "body lantern" 身燈 in the context of self-mutilation; display of lamps 放燈 in festivals. Chen refers randomly to Buddhist sutras, hinting at stories and meanings, without text references ( I doubt he understands these texts), in an attempt to explain some of the terms found in the two lantern-lighting prayers he "translated". He translates a prayer from P.2341v from the time of the Tibetan occupation, 781–848) in Dunhuang (dedicated to king Khri-Ide-srong-brtsa, r. 815–841). In sum: Chen Huaiyu focuses on three prayers in Dunhuang manuscripts ( P.2058 (6), P.2588, P.2341v) from the 9th-10th century (Guiyijun 歸義軍 period, 851–ca. 1000), which he wants to translate and to understand, but he has no idea how to work with texts, or how to annotate a translation. It is unclear why he chose these texts, and what his intentions are in translating them. He "compares" prayers "written in Tibetan (1 prayer) and Chinese (2 prayers)" without quoting the primary texts, or giving translations. It is unclear to me if he is able to read Tibetan at all.

titleInfo.title: Buddhism Across Asia. Networks of Material, Intellectual and Cultural Exchange. Volume 1
